summary: Patrick reports April 2007 stats for Bingo Card Creator, showing 26 sales and $544 profit after expenses. Easter week severely hurt sales with only one purchase during the holiday. He breaks down traffic sources, noting AdWords visitors convert best while organic Google traffic converts poorly despite being the largest segment. He observes the long tail effect where roughly 30% of Google traffic and downloads come from the top 10 search terms, with the rest spread across hundreds of niche queries.
key_points: Holiday periods like Easter cause dramatic drops in sales for education-focused software, similar to Christmas vacation effects
AdWords visitors convert at much higher rates than organic Google traffic, despite organic being the largest traffic segment
Google Analytics attribution is flawed because users who download the trial and return later get classified as 'direct' traffic rather than credited to their original source
The long tail of search is significant: only 30% of Google traffic comes from the top 10 terms, with the remaining 70% from hundreds of niche queries
Content network advertising worked surprisingly well, while advertising on a competitor's site did not
money_quotes: Week-long holidays like Easter hurt like crazy — I had exactly one sale during Holy Week.
Advertising directly on a competing site did not. Constant improvement on the main campaign did. Content network did, to my surprise.
I generally make most of my decisions based on the G2 column (i.e. free trials downloaded).
I perform fairly poorly in terms of conversion rates on the high traffic queries like 'bingo cards' and very well on long tail queries.
In a very Long Tail-esque moment, I get about 30% of my Google hits and trial downloads from the top 10 search terms.

Probably one third of my downloaders are from the top 10 search terms, one third are from less common but still fairly popular search terms, and one third are looking for a phrase that only exists for them.
key_insight: For niche software businesses, the long tail of search queries collectively drives as much value as the obvious high-volume terms, and understanding attribution across the customer journey matters more than raw traffic numbers.
